data.title: Laureate Education: Why Its Future Deserves A Premium Valuation
data.description:
Laureate Education: A 37.2% Upside with Conservative P/E Re-Rating

Laureate Education (NASDAQ:LAUR), a U.S.-based company providing higher education in Mexico and Peru. LAUR is trading at a discounted forward P/E multiple at 13.3x, which is below the discretionary sector median of 17x and peer group average of 20.1x. I’ve done a comparative analysis and assumed a conservative forward 16.6x price-to-earnings, which I think is a fair multiple compared to its closest peers ( GHC, ATGE, LOPE). Laureate has outperformed GHC and ATGE in net margin and return on equity, which has led me to assume a forward P/E that’s close to the peers. I derived a fair price of $32.5 per share, representing an upside of 37.2%. Hence, I'm rating LAUR a Strong Buy.

An Overview of Laureate Education

Laureate Education, headquartered in Miami Florida , is an international provider of higher education, operating exclusively in Mexico and Peru. The company runs two universities in Mexico, which are Universidad del Valle de México (UVM) and Universidad Tecnológica de México (UNITEC), totaling 237K enrollment as of Q2 2025. In Peru, LAUR runs three universities, which are Universidad Peruana de Ciencias Aplicadas (UPC), Universidad Privada del Norte (UPN), and CIBERTEC, totaling 234K enrollment. Here’s a brief about each university in the slide below.

Laureate Education is identified as a Public Benefit Corp. by Delaware Division of Corporations. The company has also granted the B Corp. (Benefit Corp.) by the nonprofit organization B Lab. This certification recognizes companies meeting rigorous standards of social and environmental performance. Both designations reflect Laureate’s mission toward balancing profitability with social impact.

Financials: Growth in Peru, Decline in Mexico, Navigating FX impact

Laureate Education reported mixed signals in its Q2 2025 SEC filing. It showed revenue growth and gross margin improvement, while facing hardships in its bottom line. Revenue grew by $25MM, or roughly 5%.

The growth was driven by a 9% increase in Peru, offset by a 0.55% decrease in Mexico. LAUR enjoyed organic growth in tuition and educational services revenue of 4% in Mexico and 9.8% in Peru.

The decline in Mexico’s segment revenue was attributed to a 17.8% decrease in other revenues, though it was minimal. So, Mexico’s net sales decline of 0.55% is mainly due to discounts and waivers which countered the 4.01% growth in tuition fee revenue. Foreign currency fluctuations had a negative impact on Mexico’s segment, as the weakened Mexican peso against the U.S. dollar reduced revenue by $24.2 million. In Peru, conversely, LAUR saw a net revenue growth of 9.30%.

In contrast to the good narrative of Q2, on an HoH (Half over Half) basis, revenue witnessed a slight decline of -1.84%. The decline was due to FX loss, the sale of K-12 educational assets in Mexico impacted revenue, and increased discounts and waivers, in addition to FX loss.

My take on Laureate’s revenue is that the company is very fragile to FX fluctuations. I think the company is not able to increase the number of enrollments without offering discounts, which seems to have offset the gains from new enrollments.

Gross profit grew in dollars by 14.73%, mainly due to net change in foreign currency exchange rates that saved $20MM for LAUR, as mentioned in the 10-Q. The decrease was partially offset by $19MM incurred costs due to operational changes related to the high number of enrollment at Laureate’s institutions.

Gross Margin grew by 3.35 percentage points, as a result of Peru segment margin improved by 10.5%, and Mexico Segment margin improved by 4.9%. The margin improvement is mainly due to a favorable program mix, as revenue increased without a corresponding increase in direct costs.

Net profit fell sharply by 24%. Laureate reported a loss of $25.5MM for Q2, compared to a gain of $27.4MM in the same period in 2024. This explains the decline in both net profit and margin.

Metric

Q2 2025

Q2 2024

Change

Revenue

$524.15MM

$499.24MM

Gross Profit

$206.80MM

$180.24MM

Gross Margin

39.45%

3.35%

Net Profit

$97.426MM

$128.351MM

-24.09%

Net Profit Margin

18.58%

25.70%

-7.12%

Outlook for Laureate Education: Balancing Expansion and Currency Volatility

The outlook for the full year is mixed, somehow on the same flow of the first half 2025. SA analysts are projecting revenue to grow by 3.82%, reaching $1.63B. I think the growth outlook will be driven by Laureate’s expansion. The company is expected to open two campuses by September 2025, which may increase the number of enrollments. Enrollments already increased through April 2025 by 7% in Mexico and 6% in Peru. According to the earnings call, the company will add two more campuses by late 2026 or early 2027, which will drive the long term revenue growth, as shown below.

Earnings per share are expected to decline for the full year, a YoY decline of 19.2%,

as a consequence of the decline trend. The decline is due to the reported GAAP net loss of $19.4MM, normalized to $10.7MM loss in Q1 2025. The company’s normalized net income in Q2 is still below what it made in the same period in 2024. Bear to mention that FX affects Laureate’s bottom line, especially the Mexican Peso fluctuations against the dollar, which would create surprises in the bottom line.

The online programs for working adults is one to watch in the coming quarters as a growing revenue generator for Laureate. This segment generally represents 22% of total revenue. It has a better margin than ordinary on-campus enrollment programs, as digital platforms or on-demand courses are lower in costs than on-campus education. The segment is already mature in Mexico, and management stated they’re applying the same model in Peru which I speculate will contribute to the stock’s future EPS growth.

Laureate has just launched the online degree programs in Peru in 2023 as it wasn’t permitted earlier.

Eilif Serck-Hanssen

“Strong interest by working adult students for that fully online product in Peru... mirroring what we saw in Mexico.”

Comparative Analysis: Time for a Re-Rating?

I compared Laureate Education to five similar companies chosen by SA quant.

LAUR is trading at a discounted forward P/E multiple of 14.3x its earnings compared to the 20.3x peer average. The stock is trading at a discounted multiple compared to four of its peers (COUR, GHC, ATGE, LOPE). I think Laureate’s multiple can go nowhere near Coursera’s, as COUR is a leading purely digital education provider. Bear mentioning Coursera might even be overvalued. However, when comparing Laureate to Graham Holdings, Adtalem Global, and Grand Canyon Education, these companies are not different from each other. Although they’re trading at a premium compared to LAUR.

Graham Holdings at a 20.8x forward P/E owns Kaplan, Inc., that offers educational services in nearly 30 countries through K-12 education, higher education.

Adtalem Education at a 15.9x forward P/E specializes in providing healthcare education, mainly in the U.S., and runs the American University in the Caribbean and Ross University in the Caribbean, which is exposed to similar geographic risks as Laureate Education. Finally, Grand Canyon Education (LOPE) at a 21.4x forward P/E offers supportive functions for higher education institutions.

Now that I have briefly explained GHC, ATGE, LOPE, I’m going to discuss why LAUR deserves a re-rate at a forward P/E near those stocks.

Laureate Education enjoys a lower level of indirect operating costs. Despite FX headwinds or tailwinds, the company’s trailing twelve months net income margin stands at 16.4%, which is higher than GHC 14.1%, ATGE 13.3%, and lower than LOPE 21.9%. Adding to the legit net margin, I expect Laureate’s expansion in the online degrees in Peru will add to its revenue with expanded margin. Moreover, Laureate enjoys a strong level of return on equity 26%, compared to GHC 16.1%, ATGE 16.6%, though slightly lower than LOPE 29%. Laureate carries a small net debt of $313MM, with a net debt to EBITDA ratio of 0.7x, meaning it could clear its debt with 70% of operating income.

Adding to the undervaluation case, Laureate is trading at 7.9x forward EV/EBITDA, which is a massive discount compared to the 12.9x peer average.

Company Name

Ticker

Forward P/E Non-GAAP

Forward EV/EBITDA

Laureate Education

LAUR

Graham Holdings Company

Peer Average

Valuation Summary

I believe that the market could re-rate Laureate at a higher P/E, conservatively speaking 16.3x, which is lower than the peer average by 4x. I chose 16.6x in respect to the geographic risks associated with Peru and Mexico. If the company doesn’t witness major surprises related to FX fluctuations, it’s positioned for a long term EPS growth outlook. The main drivers would be the new campuses that management declared that will be added, and expanding in the online degree programs in Peru. The company’s low debt doesn’t put stress on the bottom line and it doesn’t pay dividends, which would help in financing the campuses. By applying the conservative 16.6 forward P/E, multiplying by 2026 EPS $1.96, provided by SA, we derive a fair value of $32.5, targeting an upside potential of 29.1%. It usually takes the market 18-24 months to re-rate the stock. In my view, it would take LAUR to deliver the projected EPS growth to deserve the fair multiple compared to the peers.

Assumed Forward P/E

16.3x

Estimated EPS 2026

$1.96

Target Price

$31.9

Potential Upside

29.1%

Bearish Case:

As the company is purely operating in Mexico and Peru which are somewhat developing countries, it faces inflation or currency devaluation risks. FX fluctuations can substantially impact LAUR’s bottom line.

Additionally, any changes in government policies regarding for-profit higher education could influence Laureate’s enrollment rate.

Investor Takeaway:

I see LAUR as a Strong Buy at current P/E and EV/EBITDA levels, with a focus on medium to long-term holding. Key catalysts to watch would include the enrollment growth from the new campuses, capitalizing on the online degree programs in Peru. Currency volatility in Mexico and Peru remains the main factor that could disrupt earnings. Key risks would be monitoring FX trends, and any changes in education policy in both countries will be critical in assessing whether the re-rating thesis stays intact.

data.title: Amazon: The AWS Business Faces Challenges But The Stock Looks Appealing
data.description:
Introduction

Per my May article, Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) margins have been improving. The 2Q25 financials have come out since then along with new disclosures from AWS competitors such as Azure (MSFT). My thesis is that the AWS business faces challenges but the stock looks appealing.

The Numbers

The three hyperscale cloud companies are growing quickly so we don’t want to use trailing twelve months (“TTM”) revenue for valuation but it is useful for comparisons. Microsoft finally broke out Azure separately for their fiscal year through June in their latest release. Azure had June 2025 TTM revenue of more than $75 billion and this was up 34% which means they had TTM revenue of more than $56 billion through June 2024. Google Cloud (GOOG) (GOOGL) had June 2025 TTM revenue of $49.2 billion which was 31% higher than their June 2024 TTM revenue of $37.5 billion. AWS had June 2025 TTM revenue of $116.4 billion which was 18% higher than their June 2024 TTM revenue of $98.6 billion. Said another way, the amount of incremental revenue from the TTM was $19 billion for Azure, $17.8 billion for AWS and $11.7 billion for Google Cloud. Something is wrong at Amazon because Azure should not have more incremental revenue than AWS. Amazon faces challenges in terms of addressing this.

Seeing as AWS has a higher revenue base than their two biggest competitors, it would be understandable for AWS to have revenue growth in the 20 something percent range but it is only in the high teens which is a wide gap from Azure and Google Cloud who are both in the 30s. This challenge AWS faces in terms of preserving market share is a huge concern so it was discussed at length in the 2Q25 call. JPMorgan (JPM) Analyst Doug Anmuth was the first participant in the Q&A portion of the call and he noted how Azure and Google Cloud are growing faster than AWS. He asked about more customer demand and infrastructure supply with respect to the output gap. CEO Andy Jassy talked about the base size in his answer by saying Azure is only about 65% of the size of AWS. But as we saw above, this part of his answer isn’t satisfying because it reinforces the fact that Azure is too small to have higher incremental revenue than AWS. He said operational performances, availability, durability and latency matter and he believes AWS has advantages in those areas. He also said customers care a great deal about security (emphasis added):

They care a lot about security. If you have data that matters. And for most companies, they're putting data that they really care about in the cloud. The security and the privacy of that data matters a lot, and there are very different results in security in AWS than you'll see in other players. And yes, you could just look at what's happened in the last couple of months, you can just see, kind of, adventures at some of these players almost every month. And so a very big difference, I think, in security.

CEO Jassy didn’t mention Microsoft directly when talking about “adventures at some of these players” but the Microsoft SharePoint security flaw has been in the news quite a bit with publications such as Reuters. CEO Jassy went on to say AWS offers tremendous functionality from the bottom of the stack to the top. He emphasized how rare it is to have a run rate of $123 billion in annual revenue in a field where it is still early. Later in the call, Evercore (EVR) Analyst Mark Mahaney asked about the AWS backlog, and IR Director Dave Fildes said it was $195 billion as of June 30 which is up 25% YoY. Analyst Mahaney also asked about AWS supply constraints, and CEO Jassy said power is the biggest factor.

Staying with the cloud theme of the call, Morgan Stanley (MS) Analyst Brian Nowak asked tough questions about AWS losing share to peers while falling behind with GenAI and innovation. CEO Jassy didn’t take the question lightly as he gave an answer that was nearly 8 minutes long. He said it’s still early with AI and it’s top heavy, meaning a small number of extremely large frontier models are spending a lot on compute - a couple on AWS and others elsewhere. Additionally, the number of large-scale GenAI applications is relatively small. ChatGPT is by far the largest in the chatbot category, and then we have coding agents like Cursor, Vercel and Lovable. Cursor primarily uses AWS, and Vercel is like a layer on top of AWS. He says there are a large number of GenAI applications in these early days.

Right now much of the cost for AI customers is in training, but CEO Jassy says this will change as we move past the early days. He then says 80 to 90% of the cost for AI customers will be inference at scale because training is only done periodically while predictions and inferences happen all the time. He says in the future, customers will care more about what is offered at the different levels of the stack than they do now. He compares the upcoming situation to what happened in the CPU space with Intel (INTC). AWS designed their own custom silicon including Graviton which gives customers about 40% more price performance than external x86 processors. He sees future AI customers moving to Trainium2 the way past CPU customers moved to Graviton (emphasis added):

We've done the same thing on the custom silicon side in AI with Trainium and our second version of Trainium2 is really - it's become the backbone of Anthropic's next Claude models they're training on top of and it's become the backbone of Bedrock and the inference that we do. So I think a lot of the inference, it's about 30% and 40% better price performance than the other GPU providers out there right now, and we're already working on our third version of Trainium as well. So I think a lot of the compute and the inference is going to ultimately be run on top of Trainium2. And I think that price performance is going to matter to people as they get to scale.

The Intel comparisons are concerning because Nvidia (NVDA) is not Intel. One of the ways we see this is with respect to revenue per employee ($ in millions):

Nvidia

Intel

Amazon

2022

$1.0

$0.5

$0.3

2023

$2.1

$0.4

$0.4

2024

$3.6

$0.5

$0.4

*Nvidia’s fiscal years are off from the calendar years by a month.

Nvidia is not standing still in this respect. I think they can reach revenue of $5 million per employee for their fiscal year through January 2026 while Intel and Amazon stay pretty much flat. Nvidia eats their own cooking. Their employees use AI effectively internally to design better and better AI hardware each year. Intel was not able to do this years ago with CPUs, and AWS can’t do it as well as Nvidia with GPUs right now. I am optimistic that AWS will be smart with custom GPU silicon and not bet the ranch on it unless there are overwhelmingly positive data points that are completely separate from the CPU experience which was apples and oranges.

More examples of how AWS is facing AI challenges became evident as CEO Jassy continued answering Analyst Nowak in the call. He noted how people keep using SageMaker AI expansively, and he mentioned how Bedrock is growing substantially. After the 2Q25 call, he made a post saying OpenAI open weight models are now available on Bedrock.

Currently the number of agents at scale is small as we are in the early innings of the AI era. AWS Strands is a software development kit (“SDK”) for building an agent’s logic while AWS AgentCore is like the operating system for running an agent. While continuing his answer to Analyst Nowak’s question, CEO Jassy mentioned both in terms of making it easier for builders to construct and deploy agents at scale. Towards the end of his answer to Analyst Nowak, CEO Jassy explained that customers are less selective about where to do AI right now because we are in the early innings. He believes future customers will want to have their AI and data in the same place, and no hyperscaler has more customer data than AWS:

Remember, a lot of generative AI inference is just going to be another building block like compute, storage and database. And so people are going to actually want to run those applications close to where the other applications are running, where their data is. There's just so many more applications and data running in AWS than anywhere else.

The March 2025 ClusterMAX Rating System from SemiAnalysis awards CoreWeave (CRWV) with the top spot - platinum. The highest hyperscaler is Azure in the gold group, so their excellent TTM revenue results through June 2025 are not surprising. AWS is in the silver group, ahead of Google Cloud in the bronze group which gives me some optimism for AWS protecting its share from Google Cloud in the future.

The analysts in the 2Q25 call peppered management with questions about AWS revenue, but they didn’t ask much about AWS operating income which fell sharply from 1Q25. The fall is very noticeable in the orange dotted line in my quarterly graph:

CFO Brian Olsavsky talked about the AWS operating income fall during the call. He emphasized seasonal stock-based compensation (“SBC”), higher depreciation, and foreign exchange rates (emphasis added):

AWS operating income was $10.2 billion. We did see AWS segment margins decline from a record high of 39.5% in Q1 to 32.9% in Q2. The largest quarter-over-quarter driver of the decrease or about half is due to the seasonal step-up in stock-based compensation expense, driven by the timing of our annual compensation cycle. AWS margins also saw headwinds from higher depreciation expense as well as unfavorable impacts from year-over-year fluctuations in foreign exchange rates.

CFO Olsavsky went on to say they are increasingly investing in custom silicon like Trainium, and we know this is a sizable driver of capex and depreciation. This is an area of concern for investors. If AWS is losing share because they are overinvesting in Trainium and underinvesting in Nvidia GPUs then management should do a better job communicating why they think this is a short-term tradeoff that will be beneficial in the long run. Despite the decline in operating margin, AWS still looks like a great business, at least with respect to the accrual numbers. Everything is different if their growth capex turns out to be maintenance capex, but this is a nebulous area, and all hyperscalers face this potential issue. Per the graph above, AWS still has better margins than Google Cloud, even at similar revenue levels. For example, Google Cloud improved to an operating margin of 21% in 2Q25 with operating income of $2.8 billion on revenue of $13.6 billion. But the AWS margin was 31% in 1Q21 when they had operating income of $4.2 billion on revenue of $13.5 billion.

Both Google Cloud and AWS have advantages over Azure with respect to custom AI silicon. Google has been using TPUs for years internally, and AWS is making nice progress with Trainium. If AWS doesn’t overinvest in custom silicon then their Trainium efforts can be a big advantage over Azure in the years ahead.

Seeing as 85 to 90% of global IT spend is still on-premise, I am sanguine about the prospects for all three hyperscalers. I believe Google Cloud, Azure and AWS are all in good shape. Azure and Google Cloud have more revenue growth right now, but AWS was the first hyperscaler, and they have an excellent operating margin relative to Google Cloud, even after it dropped from 39.5% in 1Q25 to 32.9% in 2Q25.

Valuation

There is uncertainty which means risk in the AI space right now while we are in the early days. All three hyperscalers face challenges as they need more power quickly, but they also prefer green energy. Difficult decisions have to be made, particularly with natural gas. Another challenge common to all three hyperscalers is the capital expenditure requirements. A high return on investment of the growth portion of capex investments is not guaranteed. AWS in particular is vulnerable if they make poor decisions in this area with their Trainium investments. In other words, the accounting assumptions for AWS are a valuation risk. Much of the capex is for growth, so there is a large gap between the cash flow in this area and the accrual earnings. If the accounting assumptions about depreciation and amortization are incorrect then the accrual earnings for AWS are not indicative of the true earning power of AWS. Finally, AWS needs to make sure they are competitive in the long run with respect to GenAI and innovation.

The North America and international markets are getting mature enough for the TTM numbers to have some use. However, this isn’t yet the case for the faster-growing AWS segment where it is better to take the latest quarter and annualize it. These are some of the numbers I consider when thinking about the valuation range ($ in millions):

I like to look at TTM non-AWS revenue and then apply a hypothetical operating margin of 8%. We’re already close to 8% on the North America side but it will be some time before we get there on the International side. 8% of $553.7 billion TTM non-AWS revenue [$404.1 billion + $149.6 billion] comes out to $44.3 billion. I think it is reasonable to use a 30 to 35x multiple on this for a non-AWS valuation range of $1,330 to $1,550 billion when rounding to the nearest $5 billion.

2Q25 AWS operating income was $10.2 billion on revenue of $30.9 billion, and if we annualize this then it is operating income of $40.8 billion on revenue of $123.6 billion. Applying a multiple of 25 to 30x on the annualized operating income gives us an AWS valuation range of $1,020 to $1,225 billion when rounded to the nearest $5 billion.

Here is my overall valuation range:

$1,330 to $1,550 billion non-AWS

$1,020 to $1,225 billion AWS

—-----------------------------------------

$2,350 to $2,775 billion

The 2Q25 10-Q shows 10,664,912,097 shares as of July 23. Multiplying by the August 6 share price of $222.31 gives us a market cap of $2,371 billion. The market cap is near the bottom of my valuation range, and I think the stock is a buy for long-term investors who plan to hold it for at least three years.

Disclaimer: Any material in this article should not be relied on as a formal investment recommendation. Never buy a stock without doing your own thorough research.
